financial_information.family_income_for_past_year_include_your: Approximately $48,500 total household income for the past year (before taxes). This includes about $39,000 from my mother’s full-time job as a medical assistant at a family clinic in Kenton, OH; approximately $4,500 from my father’s part-time work doing seasonal construction and handyman jobs; and around $5,000 from my own part-time employment at the local grocery store in Ada, OH. We do not have any other significant sources of income, and my parents do not receive child support or alimony. Our family budget is tight due to rent, utilities, car payments, and medical expenses, so there is very little left over at the end of each month for college savings.

essay_questions.essay_would_you_enjoy_attending_and_earn:
I am planning to attend a four-year college and earn a bachelor’s degree in Business Administration with a concentration in Accounting or Finance. I have always been drawn to numbers, organization, and problem-solving, and over the last two years I have realized that I genuinely enjoy classes like Algebra II, Pre-Calculus, and Personal Finance. I also like the idea that business and finance skills are highly transferable and can open doors in many different industries.

In college, I am especially interested in studying accounting, financial management, business law, and data analysis. I want to understand how organizations make financial decisions, how budgets are created and monitored, and how to interpret financial statements in a way that helps a business or nonprofit stay healthy and grow. I am also curious about courses in marketing and communication because I know that it’s not enough just to understand the numbers—you also need to be able to explain them clearly to people who may not have a financial background.

The profession I am most interested in right now is becoming a certified public accountant (CPA) or working as a financial analyst for a mid-sized company or community organization. I would like to gain strong skills in bookkeeping, tax preparation, auditing, and financial planning. Additionally, I want to learn how to use common business software and tools such as Excel at an advanced level, QuickBooks, and data visualization or analytics platforms. Being fluent in these tools will make me more marketable in today’s business world, where employers expect new hires to be comfortable with technology and able to work efficiently with data.

Beyond the technical side, I want my schooling to help me strengthen my soft skills: written and verbal communication, teamwork, time management, and ethical decision-making. In high school, I have already seen how important it is to be able to work with others on group projects, meet deadlines even when I am busy with activities and work, and handle confidential information responsibly (for example, when I help my mom organize some of her bills at home). In college, I hope to join business clubs, volunteer with organizations that offer free tax help or budgeting workshops, and possibly complete internships that will give me hands-on experience.

If for some reason accounting or finance does not end up being the perfect fit, I am also considering related fields such as human resources, operations management, or nonprofit administration. All of these areas would still allow me to use organizational and analytical skills while serving people in a practical way. Overall, I am excited about a four-year degree because it will give me time to explore these interests in depth, gain a broad foundation in business, and build a set of skills that will make me competitive and adaptable in a changing job market.
essay_questions.essay_what_are_your_career_goals_where_d:
My long-term career goal is to become a certified public accountant (CPA) or financial professional who helps small businesses and families in my community make informed, responsible financial decisions. I want to use my skills with numbers and organization to give people more stability and peace of mind, especially those who may not have had access to financial education growing up.

In the next 5 years, I see myself completing my bachelor’s degree in Business Administration with a concentration in Accounting or Finance at a public university here in Ohio, such as Bowling Green State University or the University of Toledo. During those four years, I plan to stay focused on maintaining a strong GPA while also getting practical experience through internships or part-time work in a finance-related role. Ideally, by my junior or senior year I would like to intern in the accounting department of a local company or at a small CPA firm. That kind of real-world exposure will help me connect what I learn in class—like financial accounting, managerial accounting, and tax law—to actual day-to-day work.

In addition to academics and internships, I hope to be actively involved in at least one or two student organizations, such as a business club, Accounting Society, or a women-in-business group. I see myself attending networking events, job fairs, and workshops that teach resume-building, interviewing skills, and professional etiquette. By the time I graduate, my goal is to have a clear sense of which area of accounting or finance I want to specialize in and to have already started the process of preparing for the CPA exam if that is the route I choose.

Looking ahead 10 years, I picture myself working full-time as an accountant or financial analyst, either at a regional accounting firm, a local bank, or a mid-sized business in northwest Ohio. At that point, I would like to have earned my CPA license (if I pursue accounting) or another relevant certification, such as a Certified Management Accountant (CMA) or Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA), depending on the direction my career takes. I hope to be in a position where I am trusted to manage significant responsibilities—such as overseeing budgets, preparing and reviewing financial statements, helping clients with tax planning, or advising a company’s leadership team on financial decisions.

On a personal level, in 5 to 10 years I also see myself becoming more financially independent and stable. Coming from a family where money has always been tight, it is important to me to be able to support myself, contribute to my parents’ expenses when they get older, and possibly help my younger cousins understand budgeting and saving. I would love to eventually be in a position where I can give back to my community by volunteering my time with organizations that offer free tax preparation or financial literacy workshops, especially for low-income families or first-generation college students.

Overall, my career goals are not just about earning a good salary; they are about building a life where I can use my strengths in math, organization, and communication to help others, while also providing stability for myself and my family. College is the first big step toward that vision, and I am determined to work hard, stay focused, and take advantage of every opportunity that comes my way.
essay_questions.essay_tell_us_about_your_family_have_any:
I come from a close-knit, working-class family that has always emphasized hard work, honesty, and helping each other, even when money and resources are limited. My immediate family consists of my mom, my dad, my younger brother, and me. We live in a modest rented house in Dunkirk, Ohio, not far from Hardin Northern High School. We have never had a lot materially, but my parents have always tried to make our home a place where we support one another.

My mom, Angela Thompson, is 44 years old and works full-time as a medical assistant at a family clinic in Kenton. She completed a one-year medical assisting certificate at a technical school right after high school, but she did not attend a four-year college. She often tells me that she wishes she had had the opportunity and financial support to go further in her education because she enjoys her work but feels limited in how much she can advance. She has been the main breadwinner in our family for as long as I can remember, and she often picks up extra shifts when coworkers are out just to make sure we can cover our bills.

My dad, Mark Thompson, is 46 and works part-time doing seasonal construction jobs, handyman work, and odd jobs around the area. He completed high school but did not attend college. He was laid off from a factory job several years ago when the company downsized, and since then it has been difficult for him to find stable, full-time work with benefits. He has some ongoing back issues from years of physical labor, which sometimes limit the kind of work he can do. Despite this, he does everything he can to contribute and is the one who fixes things around the house, cooks dinner when my mom works late, and helps my brother with his homework.

I have one younger brother, Jacob, who is 13 and in middle school. He looks up to me a lot, and part of my motivation to go to college is to show him that it’s possible, even when no one in your immediate family has done it before. We talk about his interests and what he might want to study someday, and I try to include him when I’m researching colleges or filling out applications so he can start to imagine his own future.

In terms of college attendance, I will be a first-generation four-year college student in my immediate family. Neither of my parents has a bachelor’s degree, and none of my grandparents attended college. A few of my cousins have started at community colleges or technical programs, but many of them were unable to finish because of financial or family responsibilities. Because of this background, my parents are very supportive of my education emotionally, but they often feel overwhelmed by the process of applications, financial aid forms, and scholarship requirements. I have taken the lead on researching what needs to be done, meeting with my school counselor, and filling out forms like the FAFSA.

Financially, our situation is challenging. My parents’ combined income is modest, and we live paycheck to paycheck. We do not have a college savings account, and unexpected expenses—like car repairs, medical bills, or higher heating costs in the winter—can easily throw our budget off. My parents have always paid our bills on time when they can, but there have been months when we have had to choose which bills to prioritize. Because of this, I started working part-time as soon as I turned 16 to help with my own expenses like school supplies, clothes, and saving for college application fees.

Despite these challenges, my family is incredibly supportive. My mom reminds me to study and celebrates every good grade or award I receive. My dad helps me practice interview questions and always tells me he is proud of me, even if he doesn’t fully understand every part of the college process. At the same time, I feel a certain amount of pressure because I know that my success could open doors for my brother and set a new example for our family. I’m determined to make the most of this opportunity, not just for myself, but for all of us.
essay_questions.essay_please_tell_us_about_yourself_this:
I am a senior at Hardin Northern High School, and I would describe myself as a hardworking, organized, and quietly determined person. I am not always the loudest voice in the room, but I am someone who shows up, follows through, and tries to do the right thing, even when it’s difficult. Growing up in a small town like Dunkirk has taught me the value of community—people notice when you work hard, and they also notice when you help others.

Academically, I have always taken my schoolwork seriously because I see education as my path to a different future than the one my parents had. I challenge myself with the most rigorous classes available at our school, including Honors English, Pre-Calculus, and College Credit Plus courses in Composition and Intro to Business. I am proud that I have maintained strong grades while also balancing work and family responsibilities. I don’t think I am naturally brilliant at everything, but I am willing to put in the time—staying after class to ask questions, watching extra videos online to understand a concept, or reworking math problems until they make sense.

Outside of class, I am involved in several activities that are important to me. I am a member of the National Honor Society and Student Council, where I help organize events like blood drives, food drives, and school spirit activities. I also serve as treasurer for the Future Business Leaders of America (FBLA) chapter at our school. In that role, I keep track of dues, help plan fundraising events, and report on our budget during meetings. This experience has given me a small taste of real-world financial responsibility and has confirmed my interest in business and accounting.

I also work part-time at Miller’s Market in Ada, where I usually work around 15–20 hours per week during the school year and closer to 25–30 hours per week in the summer. I started as a cashier and have gradually taken on more responsibilities, such as helping with closing procedures, counting the drawer, and occasionally training new employees. Working there has taught me how to manage my time, deal with customers (even when they are frustrated or in a hurry), and show up reliably for my shifts. There have been weeks when I have gone straight from school to work and then home to do homework until late at night. While it can be exhausting, it has also made me more disciplined and appreciative of every opportunity I have.

Volunteering is another important part of my life. Through NHS and my church youth group, I have participated in community clean-up days, helped serve meals at a local soup kitchen in Kenton, and organized clothing drives. One of the most meaningful experiences for me has been helping with a free back-to-school supply drive. I remember what it feels like to worry about having enough money for notebooks and backpacks, so being able to hand a child a brand-new set of supplies and see their excitement means a lot to me. These experiences have shaped my belief that small acts of service can make a big difference for families who are struggling.

In terms of hobbies, I enjoy reading (especially realistic fiction and biographies), baking simple desserts like brownies and banana bread, and going on walks with my younger brother. I also like organizing things—my planner, my school notes, and even our kitchen cabinets at home. It may sound funny, but I genuinely enjoy taking something that feels chaotic and making it neat and understandable. I think that’s part of why I am drawn to accounting and finance: they give structure and clarity to something as complicated as money.

One school-related topic I care about is financial literacy. I believe that every high school student should graduate with at least a basic understanding of budgeting, credit, interest rates, and student loans. I have watched my parents struggle with credit card debt and confusing medical bills, and I know that many families in our community face similar challenges. I would like to use my future education to help change that—maybe by volunteering to teach workshops or by working with local schools or nonprofits to create simple, practical resources.

There are also extra circumstances that contribute to my need for financial assistance. As I mentioned earlier, my family lives on a limited income, and we do not have savings set aside for college. My parents are supportive, but they simply cannot afford to contribute much financially. On top of that, my dad’s health issues and unstable work situation mean that our financial situation can change quickly based on whether he is able to find enough hours. I have been saving as much as I can from my part-time job, but even with my savings, federal financial aid, and possible work-study, there will still be a gap when it comes to paying for tuition, books, housing, and other expenses.

Despite these challenges, I am very willing to learn and to work for what I want. I am not expecting anything to be handed to me. I am prepared to continue working part-time in college, to seek out tutoring or advising when I need help, and to stay focused on my long-term goals even when the workload is heavy. I know that college will be an adjustment, but I also know that I have already built many of the habits—like time management, perseverance, and asking for help—that I will need to succeed.

In short, I am a student who is motivated not just by grades, but by the desire to build a more stable and secure future for myself and my family. I am eager to keep learning, to contribute to my campus and community, and to make the most of any opportunity I am given.
essay_questions.essay_why_do_you_think_the_committee_sho:
I believe the committee should consider awarding this scholarship to me because I am a hardworking, first-generation college-bound student who is committed to using education to improve both my own life and the lives of those around me, but who faces significant financial barriers to attending a four-year college.

From an academic standpoint, I have consistently challenged myself with rigorous courses and maintained strong grades while balancing part-time work and family responsibilities. I do not take my education for granted, because I have seen firsthand what it looks like when opportunities are limited by finances or lack of access. Every time I stay up late finishing an assignment after a shift at work, or spend a Saturday studying instead of going out with friends, it is because I know that doing well in high school is the foundation for everything I hope to achieve in college and beyond.

I am also deeply motivated and focused on clear, realistic goals. I am not just applying to college because it is the next step; I have a specific plan to pursue a degree in Business Administration with a concentration in Accounting or Finance, and I understand how that degree connects to a future career as an accountant or financial professional. I want to help small businesses, families, and individuals in communities like ours make better financial decisions and feel less overwhelmed by money. My experiences—whether helping my mom sort through bills at the kitchen table or managing the budget as treasurer of our FBLA chapter—have shown me that responsible financial guidance can make a meaningful difference in people’s lives.

Financial need is a very real factor for my family. My parents work hard, but our household income is modest and unpredictable. We live month to month, and there is no college fund waiting for me. I have done everything I can to contribute by working 15–20 hours a week during the school year and more in the summer, saving for application fees and future college expenses. Even so, the cost of tuition, books, housing, and transportation at a four-year college is far beyond what my family can afford on our own.

This scholarship would not just make college more affordable; it would make it possible for me to focus more on my studies and less on how many extra hours I need to work each week to pay my bills. It would reduce the amount I need to borrow in student loans and allow me to take advantage of opportunities like internships, tutoring, or campus involvement that I might otherwise have to pass up in order to work more hours. In other words, your support would not be a short-term gift; it would have a long-term impact on the kind of education and experiences I can pursue.

I also believe that I would honor this scholarship by giving back. I do not see my education as something that benefits only me. I hope to return to communities like Dunkirk and Kenton and use my skills to support local families and businesses. I would like to volunteer with programs that offer free tax preparation or financial literacy workshops, particularly for low-income families and first-generation college students. I know what it feels like to be confused by financial forms and to worry about every dollar, and I want to be someone who can help lighten that burden for others.

Ultimately, I am asking the committee to invest in a student who is determined, responsible, and grateful. I will bring that same determination and gratitude with me to college classrooms, internships, and future workplaces. Receiving this scholarship would not only relieve some of the financial pressure on my family, but it would also send a powerful message to me and my younger brother that hard work and perseverance are noticed and supported. If chosen, I will work every day to live up to the trust you place in me and to make the most of the opportunity you provide.
